classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the taking of the census of the Commonwealth, and for other purposes," approved March 18, 1876, and to provide for the enrollment of voters.
classification.summary: This act mandates that tax assessors create a separate column in their books to enroll the names of citizens entitled to vote for Representative in 1881. The enrollment is to be based on the assessor's personal knowledge, information directly from the person, or sworn testimony from others. Assessors are subject to a fine for improperly enrolling names and must follow existing laws to prevent illegal voting. They are also required to take an oath to fairly ascertain qualified voters and report the numbers. Assessors are empowered to examine potential voters under oath.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the enrollment of voters and the process by which individuals are deemed qualified to vote. While it does not explicitly mention race, voter registration laws during the Jim Crow era were often used as a tool to disenfranchise Black voters through various means such as poll taxes, literacy tests, and grandfather clauses, which were not explicitly racial but had a discriminatory effect. This statute's focus on the assessor's knowledge and the process of qualification could have been used in conjunction with other, unstated or implicitly understood, criteria that disproportionately affected Black citizens. Therefore, it is ambiguous and requires human review to determine if it was part of a broader system of voter suppression.

classification.title: An Act to Aid in the Erection of Buildings for the Lexington College
classification.summary: This act authorizes the city of Lexington to issue bonds to fund the erection of buildings for the Lexington College. The bonds are to be between $500 and $1,000, with 6% interest payable semi-annually, and a principal due in 30 years. The city is required to create a sinking fund for repayment, funded by taxes on express offices, telegraph offices, lottery offices, lawyers, doctors, dentists, and photographers. The land and buildings will revert to the city if the college is removed or abandoned for three consecutive years within the city limits.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the funding and construction of buildings for the Lexington College. It details the financial mechanisms, including bond issuance and taxation, to support this endeavor. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from 1880.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to authorize the city of Lexington to issue bonds for the purpose of grading and macadamizing streets, and for other purposes," approved March 10, 1876, and to levy and collect taxes for the payment of the same.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the city of Lexington. It authorizes the city to levy an additional tax for the payment of bonds, with the power to enforce payment by prohibiting the keeping of offices or practicing professions until the tax is paid. Crucially, it explicitly states that the taxes authorized by the fourth section shall only be levied and collected from the whites of said city and white property owners. The second part of the provided text concerns changes to court dates in Greenup County and is unrelated to the Lexington tax act.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly states in Section 6 that the taxes authorized by the fourth section 'shall only be levied and collected from the whites of said city, and from the white owners of property situated therein.' This is a clear indication of racial discrimination, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws, by singling out and exclusively taxing white individuals and property owners for city revenue. The rest of the text in the provided snippet pertains to a different act regarding court dates in Greenup County and is irrelevant to the classification of the Lexington tax act.

classification.title: AN ACT to grant the consent of the State of Kentucky to the acquisition by the United States of certain land within the State, and bordering on the navigable rivers thereof, for the purpose of constructing canals, or of erecting thereon dams, abutments, locks, lock-keepers' dwellings, offices, and all necessary structures for the construction and maintenance of slack-water navigation on said rivers, and ceding jurisdiction over the same, and for imposing fines and penalties for willful injuries to the grounds, buildings, and appurtenances acquired under the provisions of this act.
classification.summary: This act grants Kentucky's consent for the United States to acquire land within the state for the purpose of improving navigable rivers through canals, dams, locks, and other structures. It also cedes jurisdiction over these acquired lands to the United States concurrently with the state and establishes penalties for willful damage to these properties. The act outlines the process for land condemnation if agreement cannot be reached with landowners.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the acquisition of land by the United States for river navigation improvements and the cession of jurisdiction over that land. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. Confidence is high because the statute's purpose is clearly defined and unrelated to racial matters.

classification.title: An Act to Authorize the Issuance of Bonds for the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Kentucky
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Fayette County to issue bonds to fund the purchase of land or the erection of buildings for the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Kentucky. The proceeds are to be used solely for these purposes, with the title vested in the Commonwealth of Kentucky as long as the college uses the property. The county court is empowered to negotiate the sale of bonds and appoint agents. A tax will be levied on property subject to state taxation to cover the interest and principal of the bonds. The sheriff is responsible for collecting these taxes, and a receiver will be appointed annually.

classification.title: AN ACT regulating the city court of Louisville, and the remuneration of its officers.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act establishing a new charter for the city of Louisville. It specifies that the city court of Louisville shall be a court of record, composed of a judge, clerk, prosecuting attorney, and marshal. It also outlines the qualifications for these officers, which are to be the same as those for a county judge, county clerk, Commonwealth's attorney, and sheriff, respectively. The act further states that these officers shall be elected by the qualified voters of the city at the same time as state elections.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of the city court of Louisville and the qualifications and election of its officers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The qualifications are based on existing county and state judicial/administrative roles, which were not inherently race-based in their definition at this time, though their application could have been. However, the statute itself does not contain any racial component. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Establish a City Court in the City of Louisville"
classification.summary: This act details the jurisdiction and powers of a city court in Louisville, Kentucky. It outlines the court's original and exclusive jurisdiction over unindictable misdemeanors, its power to arrest individuals charged with felonies, hear evidence, and commit or discharge them, and its authority to hear complaints for breaches of the peace and violations of city ordinances and state penal laws. The act specifies that the judge cannot impose fines exceeding twenty dollars without a jury, and that the judge and prosecuting attorney have a duty to examine all felony cases within the city limits, acting as an examining court.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the judicial powers and jurisdiction of a city court in Louisville, Kentucky. It covers general criminal and misdemeanor proceedings, including arrest, bail, and trial procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is on the structure and function of the court system for all citizens within the city. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to authorize the Fayette county court to issue the bonds of said county, the proceeds to be used for the purchase of lands or the erection of buildings for the use of the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Kentucky," approved January 24, 1880.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act from January 24, 1880, concerning the issuance of bonds by the Fayette county court for the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Kentucky. The amendment specifies that the county court can authorize bond issuance either at its regular court of claims (with a majority of justices present and concurring) or at a special court called by the judge, to which all justices of the peace are summoned and a majority must be present and concur. It also states that bonds issued under the original act will bear interest from their date of issue, and until sold, this interest will be paid annually to the college for the temporary use of lands.
keywords: Agricultural and Mechanical College of Kentucky
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns financial matters related to a college and the issuance of county bonds. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is an amendment to existing Kentucky law concerning the role of an 'Elisor'. An elisor is an officer appointed to perform duties of a sheriff or other officer when the latter is disqualified. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It grants discretion to the court of claims regarding compensation for equipment and drivers used on roads. Surveyors are authorized to contract for services like hauling and plowing, and may exempt individuals from road work in exchange for the use of their teams and services. The act requires submission to a popular vote in Butler County for approval before taking effect.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to road maintenance and labor in Butler County, Kentucky. It outlines procedures for compensating individuals who provide equipment and services for road work and allows for exemptions from manual labor in exchange for such contributions. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about road labor and has no racial component.

classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the acquisition of land by the United States for river navigation improvements on the Kentucky River. It clarifies that the US government will have full title and jurisdiction over these acquired lands, while the State of Kentucky reserves the right to execute lawful process within these areas. The amendment specifically removes the phrase "concurrently with the State of Kentucky" and inserts language to confer full title and jurisdiction to the US government, while also specifying that the original act applies only to the Kentucky River.

It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled 'An Act to provide for the erection of buildings for the accommodation of the convicts of the State Penitentiary, approved March 19, 1878,' and to make further appropriations for the same.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the erection of buildings for the State Penitentiary. It outlines the duties of commissioners in advertising for bids, awarding contracts, and making payments for construction and repairs. Notably, it mandates the Keeper of the Penitentiary to provide twenty-five able-bodied male convicts for labor on these improvements, with the state covering their board and guard costs. The act also specifies that the convict labor will be furnished free of expense to the contractor. Additionally, it addresses the payment for children attending an institution, stating that the State will only pay for maintenance to the extent that parents are unable to do so.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the management and construction of penitentiary buildings and the use of convict labor. While it mentions 'male convicts,' there is no explicit or implicit racial classification or segregation mentioned within the text. The provisions regarding payment for children attending an institution also do not appear to have a racial component. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act authorizes the United States government to purchase and hold grounds within Owensboro, Kentucky, for federal buildings such as a post office and collector's office. It also cedes exclusive jurisdiction over these grounds to the United States, except for the administration of Kentucky's criminal laws and the service of civil process, and exempts the grounds from state and county taxes.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the adornment and utilization of the public grounds in the city of Frankfort.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the board of councilmen of Frankfort to appropriate funds for the adornment and utilization of public grounds. It establishes a committee of five citizens (three gentlemen, two ladies) to manage the park. The act also defines misdemeanors related to damaging park property, playing games, or consuming alcohol within the park, with penalties of fines or imprisonment. The police court of Frankfort has jurisdiction over violations, and the city marshal and policemen are tasked with surveillance. The act takes effect upon passage, subject to the approval of the State Librarian.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the management and regulation of public grounds in Frankfort. It outlines rules for the use and adornment of these grounds, including prohibitions on damaging property, playing certain games, and consuming alcohol. The penalties are applied generally to any person violating the rules. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section10, article17, chapter 29, of the General Statutes, title "Crimes and Punishments."
classification.summary: This act amends a section of the General Statutes concerning crimes and punishments. Specifically, it prohibits saloon-keepers, coffee-house keepers, and other dealers in spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors from selling, giving away, or loaning any of these liquors to any person on Sunday. The penalty for violation is a fine between $20 and $50, at the discretion of the jury. This act applies only to the counties of Knox, Whitley, and Pulaski.
classification.reasoning: The statute in question, Chapter 893, is an amendment to existing criminal statutes that prohibits the sale of alcoholic beverages on Sundays. While it is a criminal law and was enacted during the Jim Crow era, it does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. The restrictions apply to all persons and all dealers of liquor within the specified counties. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

It details the allowances for superintendents and stewards, including furnished quarters and meals, and outlines penalties for the private use of institutional funds. It also specifies the duties of superintendents in examining claims and reporting irregularities to the board of commissioners. The act takes effect on April 1, 1880.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to regulate the fees of county and city officers, and to provide for the payment of the same, approved March 11, 1876."
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law regarding the fees of county and city officers. classification.title: AN ACT authorizing the Auditor of Public Accounts to appoint collectors of the revenue under certain contingencies.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Auditor of Public Accounts to appoint a collector of state revenue for any county where there is no sheriff, or the sheriff has failed to give bond, or no collector has been appointed by the county court. The appointed collector must execute bond and take the constitutional oath, and will have the same powers and be subject to the same penalties as sheriffs or collectors in regard to the collection of state revenue.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the appointment of revenue collectors by the Auditor of Public Accounts when a county sheriff is absent or unqualified. It outlines the process for appointing a collector, requiring them to take an oath and provide a bond, and grants them the same powers as sheriffs in collecting state revenue. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply generally to all counties and potential collectors. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend chapter 95 of General Statutes, title "Salaries."
classification.summary: This act amends Chapter 95 of the General Statutes concerning salaries. It establishes annual salaries for various state officers and judges, payable monthly from the Treasury. Specific salaries are listed for the Assistant Secretary of State, Librarian, Interpreter of Jefferson Circuit Court, Judges of the Court of Appeals, Chancellor and Vice Chancellor at Louisville, Chancellor in Campbell County, Judge of a Circuit Court, Judge of a Criminal Court (with a provision for current officeholders), and Judge of a Common Pleas Court. It also abolishes the office of second clerk in the Register of the Land Office. The act specifies when its provisions take effect for different judicial offices.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct legislative acts. The first act amends Section 435 of the Civil Code of Practice, relating to the execution of bonds by distributees or legatees before receiving their share. The second act, titled 'AN ACT to amend chapter 95 of General Statutes, title "Salaries."', establishes annual salaries for various state officers and judges. Neither of these acts contains any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or are characteristic of Jim Crow laws. They deal with civil procedure and public official salaries, respectively. Therefore, the classification is 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, category as null, and racial_indicator as 'none'. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the header 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the structure of the acts. No human review is needed.

It outlines the process for assigning cases to this new court (every third petition) and defines the jurisdiction and duties of the Vice Chancellor, as well as the interaction with the existing chancellors and judges. It also repeals certain sections of a previous act related to juries.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the administrative and procedural organization of courts in Louisville and Jefferson County, Kentucky. It establishes a 'Vice Chancellor's Court' and defines its jurisdiction and operational procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an act passed by the General Assembly in 1880. The confidence is high because the text clearly describes court procedures without any racial component.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section550 ot an act, entitled"An act regulating practicein civilcases," approved March 20th, 1876 .
classification.summary: This act amends subsection 2 of section 550 of an act regulating practice in civil cases. It allows for an affidavit for a warning order to be made by any one of two or more plaintiffs if they are in the county, provided the affidavit states the affiant's belief that other plaintiffs residing in the county are also ignorant of the facts unknown to the affiant.

classification.title: AN ACT to provide for the transfer of civil suits at law or in equity, motions, and special proceedings in civil matters, in the circuit courts of McCracken, Ballard, and Hickman to the common pleas courts of said counties of McCracken, Ballard, and Hickman
classification.summary: This act mandates that clerks of the circuit courts in McCracken, Ballard, and Hickman counties transfer all pending civil suits, motions, appeals, and special proceedings to the common pleas courts of those respective counties. The common pleas courts will then docket and proceed with these cases as if they were originally filed there. The act specifies that it takes effect thirty days after passage and does not apply to cases where a judgment has already been rendered in equity.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the procedural transfer of civil cases between different courts within specific counties in Kentucky. It does not mention race, nor does it establish any separate facilities, differential penalties, or restrictions on rights that would indicate a Jim Crow law. The act is purely administrative and procedural in nature, relating to court jurisdiction and case management. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The racial indicator is 'none' as there is no mention or implication of race. Human review is not needed.

Violators face a fine of not less than one hundred dollars nor more than five hundred dollars for each convict introduced.

It details their authority to examine the condition and management of railroads, ensure compliance with laws, and prosecute violations. They can issue subpoenas, and failure to comply with a subpoena is a misdemeanor punishable by fine or imprisonment.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the appointment of Railroad Commissioners, and to define their powers and duties.
classification.summary: This act establishes a board of Railroad Commissioners tasked with overseeing railroad transportation in Kentucky. The commissioners are required to report annually to the Governor on the workings of the railroad system, its impact on business and prosperity, and to make recommendations regarding fare and freight classifications. They can also investigate specific railroad matters at the Governor's direction. The act grants commissioners the right to travel on all railroads and railroad trains for their duties and prohibits them from having any financial interest in or employment with railroad companies during their term. The Governor has the power to remove commissioners and fill vacancies. Commissioners are compensated seven dollars per day when actively engaged.
classification.reasoning: The statute establishes a commission to regulate railroads. It details reporting requirements, investigative powers, and compensation for commissioners. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to prevent fraud in the manufacture and sale of commercial fertilizers in this Commonwealth.
classification.summary: This act requires individuals manufacturing and selling commercial fertilizers to report samples and ingredient lists to the Commissioner of Agriculture. It establishes penalties for failing to comply, selling without proper disclosure, or misrepresenting fertilizer composition. A certificate from the Commissioner is required for sale, and failure to obtain it is also a misdemeanor. Sellers must affix their name or trade mark to each package.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act entitled "An Act to establish the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Kentucky," approved March 21, 1878
classification.summary: This act amends the establishment of the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Kentucky. It modifies the collegiate period, the establishment of a normal department for teachers, and the admission criteria for students. Specifically, it changes the number of students that can be sent to the college free of tuition from each legislative representative district and allows for the admission of other students on a fee-paying basis, regardless of place of residence or birth. The board of trustees is empowered to establish regulations for the government and physical training of students.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided details amendments to the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Kentucky's operational and admission policies. It discusses the length of the collegiate period, the establishment of a normal department for teacher training, and changes to student admissions, including free tuition for students from legislative districts and the admission of other students based on fees, irrespective of their origin. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it refers to amending a previous act passed by the General Assembly.

classification.summary: This act regulates lotteries in Kentucky. It requires that any person or corporation selling lottery or policy tickets must first post the authorizing law at the place of sale and read it to any illiterate purchaser. Selling lottery tickets without express legal authority is prohibited. Violators face a fine of one hundred dollars, with penalties for non-payment including being hired out to hard labor, with doubled terms and jail time for escape.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to establish a State Board of Health and to define its duties."
classification.summary: This act amends an existing law to further define the powers and duties of the State Board of Health, particularly concerning the prevention and control of infectious and contagious diseases. It grants the board authority to conduct sanitary inspections, establish and enforce quarantine measures, and regulate transportation companies to prevent the spread of diseases. The board can establish quarantine grounds, erect temporary buildings, and appoint physicians and assistants to manage quarantine stations. Transportation companies are mandated to comply with the board's rules and regulations.

It grants broad powers to the board to prevent the introduction and spread of infectious diseases. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The regulations apply universally to all individuals and transportation methods to protect public health. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 102 of the General Statutes, Relating to the Public Health
classification.summary: This act amends existing public health statutes, focusing on the establishment and duties of local and state boards of health. It outlines powers related to sanitary regulations, disease prevention (cholera, small-pox, yellow fever, scarlet fever, diphtheria, and other epidemic diseases), inspection of premises, and enforcement of state board rules. Physicians and heads of families are required to report diseases. Local boards are to report to the State Board of Health quarterly. The act also grants local boards in border counties the power to declare and maintain quarantine against contagious or infectious diseases from other states or counties.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend chapter 38, article13, section6, General Statutes, title "Executions."
classification.summary: This act amends existing statutes regarding executions, attachments, distress warrants, and fee-bills. It provides an additional exemption for mechanics who are bona fide housekeepers with families, are residents of the Commonwealth, and are actively engaged in their mechanical business. This exemption allows for materials and manufactured articles, or articles in process of manufacture, up to a value of two hundred dollars. However, this specific exemption for mechanics means they cannot claim the exemption of two work beasts, one wagon, and a set of gear. The value of the exempted materials is to be determined by three disinterested housekeepers sworn by the officer making the levy.

classification.title: Jury fee
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and fees for demanding a jury trial in Kentucky civil and criminal cases. It specifies that both plaintiffs and defendants must pay a four-dollar jury fee within a certain timeframe after the summons or filing of the petition/answer, and submit a written demand. The fee paid by the successful party is taxed as costs, unless both parties demanded a jury, in which case the fee is returned to the successful party and not taxed. The statute also details how cases should be assigned for trial to ensure jury panels are not required on days set aside for court trials, and that civil cases are given precedence in assignment during the first three days of a term, with criminal cases generally having precedence otherwise.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the procedural requirements and fees for demanding a jury trial in Kentucky courts. It details the financial obligations and written demands required from parties wishing to have a jury hear their case, as well as how cases are scheduled. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: AN ACT to amend chapter 49, General Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends Chapter 49 of the General Statutes of Kentucky. It clarifies that county judges cannot exercise certain authorities during circuit or criminal court sessions, assigning those exclusively to the court. It also modifies rules for employing guards for prisoners being transported, requiring at least two prisoners for a guard and an additional guard for every two more prisoners. Sections 3 and 5 of Chapter 49 are repealed, and an officer's allowance for conveying a prisoner is set at ten cents per mile.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details amendments to existing Kentucky General Statutes concerning judicial authority during court sessions, the employment of guards for transporting prisoners, and repeal of certain sections. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is on procedural and administrative matters within the legal and penal systems. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act details the management and hiring out of convict labor from the Kentucky Penitentiary. It outlines procedures for private contracts, public bids, and the conditions under which contractors can use convict labor for agricultural production or transportation of supplies. It also addresses the Commissioners' authority to require additional security for contractor bonds and to furnish reading material for prisoners. If convict labor cannot be hired out, the Commissioners may lease shops and machinery. The act also specifies that hired prisoners remain under prison police and government, and that non-convicts are prohibited from associating with them.

classification.title: An Act to amend and reduce into one the several acts in relation to the Penitentiary.
classification.summary: This act details the operational rules and salaries for the Kentucky Penitentiary. It mandates the printing and posting of rules, including those for good conduct commutation. It also outlines the classification of prisoners to separate hardened criminals from youthful ones, provided it does not interfere with the hiring of convict labor. The act specifies salaries for commissioners, the Warden, Deputy Warden, Physician, Chaplain, Clerk, and guards. It also prohibits prison officials from having any financial interest in contracts for supplies or materials used in the prison or by contractors. Finally, it requires the Warden and Clerk to make annual settlements with the Commissioners.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the administration and operation of the Kentucky Penitentiary. It covers aspects like prisoner classification, staff salaries, and financial oversight. While it mentions classifying prisoners to separate 'old and hardened criminals' from 'youthful criminals,' there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The primary focus is on prison management and financial regulations. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the erection of a Branch Penitentiary, and for other purposes," approved March 19, 1878.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund to hire out the labor of convicts in excess of 600 from the Kentucky Penitentiary for employment on public works within the state. It specifies that certain convicts (murderers, rapists, arsonists, or those with sentences longer than five years) are not to be hired out. It also includes restrictions on where these convicts can be worked or quartered, prohibiting work within city limits and requiring a distance from private residences unless consent is given.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the employment of convicts in the Penitentiary, and for other purposes," approved March 17, 1876.
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation regarding the employment of convicts. It specifies minimum squad sizes for hired convicts, mandates an inspector for each squad to ensure contractor compliance with treatment requirements, and outlines the distribution of net proceeds from convict labor (three-fourths to the state, one-fourth to the convict). It also grants contractors, guards, and employees peace officer powers to arrest individuals interfering with convicts and details penalties for escapes and attempted escapes, including forfeiture of earnings and potential return to the penitentiary.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the management and labor of convicts. While it does not explicitly mention race, the historical context of Jim Crow laws in Kentucky (1865-1966) strongly suggests that such statutes governing the penal system, particularly convict labor, were disproportionately applied to and impacted Black individuals. The harsh penalties, the granting of peace officer powers to contractors and guards, and the forfeiture of earnings are all mechanisms that were historically used to control and exploit Black populations under the guise of penal reform. Therefore, it is classified as 'yes' for Jim Crow with implicit racial indication. The confidence is high due to the context of the archive and the nature of convict labor laws during this period. The category is 'incarceration' as it directly relates to the penal system and convict management. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the source 'GenAssemb V1-1880.pdf' and the phrasing 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY'.

classification.title: An Act to amend and re-enact Chapter 109 of the General Statutes, entitled 'Penitentiary'
classification.summary: This statute outlines the responsibilities of contractors and the Commissioners of the Sinking Fund regarding the hiring out of convicts from the Kentucky Penitentiary. It details the contractor's duty to cover transportation costs and return convicts, and authorizes the Commissioners to contract with the U.S. Government for convict labor on navigable streams if the contract is financially beneficial. An inspector is to be appointed to oversee these arrangements, with provisions for their oath, bond, salary, and the appointment of assistants for guarding and safekeeping the convicts.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the management and hiring out of convicts from the Kentucky Penitentiary. While it pertains to incarceration and labor, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The provisions apply to all convicts regardless of their race. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the context 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year 1880, indicating it's an act passed by the legislature.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to provide for the employment of convicts in the penitentiary of this Commonwealth."
classification.summary: This act details the management and hiring of convicts, specifically outlining the role of an inspector and commissioners in contracting convict labor to the United States government or its agents. It specifies conditions for the hire, including ensuring convicts are not overworked or treated inhumanely, and mandates that the commissioners ensure wages will be paid promptly. The act also empowers the commissioners to provide transportation and initial funds for the convicts.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the management and labor of convicts. While it does not explicitly mention race, the historical context of Jim Crow laws in Kentucky (1865-1966) strongly suggests that such statutes regarding convict labor were disproportionately applied to Black individuals, who were often incarcerated at higher rates due to discriminatory practices and laws. The focus on 'convicts' and their 'hire' to government entities aligns with the widespread practice of convict leasing, which was a significant tool of racial oppression during the Jim Crow era. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law due to its implicit racial implications within the historical context. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY'.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to provide for liens for laboringmen and supply men," approved March 20, 1876.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous law concerning liens for laboring men and supply men. It repeals a provision that allowed a prior lien to those who furnished materials to manufacturing establishments and instead allows a lien to all laboring men employed in such establishments for wages due within sixty days before any assignment.

It focuses on financial claims and does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: AN ACT to regulate the taking up of property found adrift upon certain rivers in this Commonwealth.
classification.summary: This act establishes fees and procedures for salvaging boats, timber, rafts, platforms, and logs found adrift on the Ohio, Kentucky, Licking, and Little Sandy rivers. It outlines the charges for taking up such property, the lien the taker-up has, and the process for sale if the owner does not claim the property within one year. It also specifies the duties of constables or other officers involved in the sale and penalties for failure to comply.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of salvaged property found adrift on specific rivers in Kentucky. It details the fees, liens, and sale procedures for such property. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clear and unrelated to racial discrimination.

classification.title: AN ACT in relation to sheriff's fees for serving process from other counties.
classification.summary: This act states that sheriffs are not required to execute or return writs, summons, or other process sent from another county unless accompanied by the legally allowed fee for serving it. It also outlines conditions for when a party against whom the process is issued cannot be found.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct acts. The first act, titled 'Penalty for maliciously throwing stone or other missile into railroad train,' deals with criminal penalties for endangering railroad passengers. The second act, 'AN ACT in relation to sheriff's fees for serving process from other counties,' addresses the administrative procedures for sheriffs handling legal documents from different counties. Neither of these acts, based on their text and titles, exhibits characteristics of Jim Crow laws, which were designed to enforce racial segregation and discrimination. They do not mention race, create separate facilities, restrict rights based on race, or have any implicit racial dimension. Therefore, both acts are classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence. The second act is chosen for classification as it is the latter and more complete act presented on the page. classification.title: AN ACT to amend sections 1, 2, 4, and 5, of chapter 49, General Statutes, title "Guards, &c."
classification.summary: This act amends Chapter 49 of the General Statutes concerning guards for prisoners. It reduces the compensation for guards from $1.50 to $1.00 per day. It also modifies the rules regarding the number of guards allowed for transporting prisoners, generally limiting it to one guard per officer unless a court order specifies otherwise. For multiple prisoners being transported to the penitentiary, the number of guards is limited to one for every two criminals, again with the possibility of court-ordered exceptions. The act also clarifies that compensation for officers and guards is for one trip only.
classification.reasoning: The statute amends existing laws regarding the employment and compensation of guards for prisoners. It focuses on the number of guards allowed and their pay, particularly when transporting prisoners to the penitentiary. There is no mention of race or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates the subject matter and lack of racial component.

classification.summary: This act amends section 756 of the Code of Practice in civil cases. It requires appellants and cross-appellants to specify their assignment of errors, and the court will only pass upon those specified errors. Judgments will not be reversed or modified except for errors that prejudice the substantial rights of the complaining party. The Court of Appeals may allow the filing of additional record portions upon motion and notice.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend and reduce into one the several acts organizing and regulating the lunatic asylums of the State," approved March 20, 1876,and the acts amendatory thereof.
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation concerning lunatic asylums. It requires the president of the board of commissioners and the superintendent of each asylum to certify the number of "idiots, epileptic imbeciles, and harmless, incurable lunatics" who can be returned to their counties of residence. The Auditor of Public Accounts is instructed not to issue warrants for the support of any patient if these individuals are not returned. Pauper idiots, epileptics, and harmless, incurable lunatics are to be returned to their respective counties and delivered to their friends or, if none, to the county judges for suitable provision from an existing appropriation.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the management and transfer of individuals deemed "idiots, epileptic imbeciles, and harmless, incurable lunatics" within the state's lunatic asylums. It mandates their return to their home counties and outlines procedures for their care. classification.title: An Act to Regulate the Sale of Liquors by Druggists
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for druggists to obtain a license to sell liquors. It requires notice to the county attorney and public posting, proof of good character and being a bona fide dealer in drugs, and a bond to ensure compliance with liquor laws. It also specifies that liquors can only be sold upon a physician's prescription and details penalties for violations, particularly in areas where liquor sales are prohibited.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of liquor sales by druggists. It details licensing procedures, requirements for applicants, and penalties for violations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The focus is on the regulation of a specific trade and the sale of a controlled substance. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act authorizes the Governor, Auditor of Public Accounts, and State Treasurer to form a board of commissioners. This board is empowered to borrow up to $500,000 from various sources (Sinking Fund Commissioners, banks, corporations, or individuals) at an interest rate not exceeding six percent per annum to cover any potential deficiencies in state revenue for ordinary government expenses over the next two years. The act also outlines procedures for repaying any borrowed funds using surplus revenue that accumulates in the Treasury.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial management of the state government, specifically authorizing the borrowing of funds to cover potential revenue shortfalls. classification.title: AN ACT to regulate the appellate jurisdiction of the courts of this Commonwealth.
classification.summary: This act outlines the appellate jurisdiction of the Court of Appeals in Kentucky. It specifies that the Court of Appeals has jurisdiction over final orders and judgments in civil cases from all other courts, with exceptions. It also states that no appeal can be taken to the Court of Appeals from a judgment for the recovery of money or personal property if the value in controversy is less than one hundred dollars, nor to reverse a judgment granting a divorce or punishing contempt.

There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or effect against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Chapter 45 of the General Statutes, Relating to Factors and Agents
classification.summary: This act clarifies the legal standing of factors and agents in relation to merchandise entrusted to them. It defines who is considered the true owner for the purpose of valid contracts, outlines the rights of those taking merchandise as security for antecedent debts, and specifies conditions under which sales or dispositions are not valid (e.g., if the buyer has notice of the agent acting outside their authority). It also clarifies that common carriers and warehousemen not acting as factors or dealers are not authorized to sell or hypothecate merchandise, and that owners can recover merchandise under certain conditions.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to establish the Institution for the Education and Training of Feeble-minded Children," approved February 23, 1874, and the acts amendatory thereof.
classification.summary: This act amends the 1874 law establishing an institution for feeble-minded children. It mandates that the medical superintendent return pupils whose mental condition is deemed not beneficial for further education to their county judges. This category includes "idiots and imbeciles" and those with unusually slow progress in learning to read and write. The superintendent is also prohibited from admitting such individuals in the future and must report on any remaining pupils to the Auditor of Public Accounts.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the management and discharge of students from an institution for "feeble-minded children." The criteria for return or non-admission are based on mental condition and educational progress, not race. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clear and does not suggest any racial discrimination.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend chapter 66, article 2, of General Statutes, title "Landlord and Tenant," to apply to the counties of Daviess, Hancock, and McLean.
classification.summary: This act amends existing statutes concerning landlord and tenant relations, specifically regarding liens on the produce of rented land used for cultivating tobacco. It requires landlords to file a recorded statement with the county clerk by September 1st to maintain a lien on tobacco or its proceeds after it has been removed from the premises. This lien would be valid for sixty days after removal against the tenant, sub-tenant, purchaser, or any recipient of the tobacco.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the revenue laws
classification.summary: This act, effective from its passage until November 1, 1881, and applying only to Kenton County, mandates the Auditor to create a list of lands sold for taxes or state indebtedness where the equity of redemption has expired. The Auditor is to publish this list and a notice of sale in a local newspaper or handbills, with copies posted publicly, at least four weeks before the sale. The sale will be conducted by the Auditor's agent at the county courthouse, selling only enough land to cover the outstanding taxes, interest, and charges.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the amendment of revenue laws, specifically the process for selling land due to delinquent taxes or state indebtedness. It outlines procedures for listing, publishing notices, and conducting sales of such lands. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the Kentucky Agricultural and Mechanical Association. It changes the date for the election of officers from the second Saturday in May to the second Saturday in February. Two weeks' notice is required for all stockholder meetings, to be published in Lexington newspapers.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the Farmers' Mutual Aid Association of Lewis county
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Farmers' Mutual Aid Association of Lewis County, Kentucky. The association is formed for the purpose of insuring dwelling houses, barns, and other buildings in Lewis County against loss by fire, wind, or lightning. It outlines the corporators, the name of the association, its powers, the management structure (a board of nine officers), and rules regarding insurance policies, including restrictions on insuring buildings already insured by other companies and a limit on policy issuance to two-thirds of the cash value. Members are obligated to pay their portion of losses and expenses, with insured buildings and land pledged as security.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act incorporating the town of Rocky Hill, Edmonson county."
classification.summary: This act amends the incorporation of the town of Rocky Hill, Edmonson county. It outlines procedures for filling vacancies in the offices of police judge and marshal, and for trustees. It also details how fines for drunkenness, disorderly conduct, or ordinance infractions can be paid through confinement or labor on town streets and public works. The act also allows the police judge to try ordinance violations without a jury if the penalty is $20 or less, and designates the town marshal as the ex officio keeper of the station-house.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to charter the Louisville Baptist Orphans' Home, approved January 28, 1870.
classification.summary: This act amends an earlier charter for the Louisville Baptist Orphans' Home. It allows the corporation to permit suitable persons to adopt children in its custody and control, or to bind them out as apprentices. The adoption process requires a written instrument, executed and acknowledged like a deed, and recorded in Jefferson County. The adopted child becomes the heir-at-law of the adopter, who gains parental control and responsibilities. The corporation retains the right to reclaim the child if it is an infant and covenants in the adoption instrument are violated.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Kentucky Grangers 'Mutual Benefit Society," approved March 7th, 1876.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Kentucky Grangers' Mutual Benefit Society. It specifies that the amount paid on any death capital stock shall not exceed three thousand dollars ($3,000). Until membership reaches three thousand, the amount paid on each death will equal the number of members. The membership may be unlimited, and all members can be in the same class, though the board of directors can issue certificates for half the amount specified in the first section.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act , entitled " An act to amend an act to reduce into one, amend , and digest the acts and amendatory acts incorporating the town of North Middletown , in Bourbon county," approved February 16, 1874 .
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the town of North Middletown. It modifies the election process for the town judge and marshal, specifying election dates, terms of office, and procedures for filling vacancies. The judge is elected every two years, and the marshal is elected annually. Vacancies for the judge are filled by election if more than a year remains in the term, otherwise by the trustees. Vacancies for the marshal are always filled by the trustees. The act also repeals any conflicting parts of a March 28th, 1872 act.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and reduce into one the acts relating to roads in Logan county.
classification.summary: This act amends and consolidates previous legislation concerning roads in Logan County. It outlines a process where if a specific act for Meade and other counties (including Logan) regarding road improvement is not adopted by Logan County by its October 1880 court of claims, then a prior act from March 15th, 1878, concerning roads in Union and Henderson counties, will apply to Logan County. However, this prior act will only be enforced if a majority of the justices in commission at the October meeting approve it. If approved, the act will be enforced under the same officers, rules, regulations, and penalties as the 1878 act.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the working of roads in certain counties
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for road maintenance in certain counties, including the appointment of superintendents, the assessment of taxes for road work, and the payment of laborers. It specifies that local residents assessed for taxation should be given preference for employment on roads within their voting precincts, provided they can be employed at the same price as other equally efficient workers. Certificates issued to laborers for their work can be used to pay their road taxes.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1880 details the process for tax assessment and supervision within a town. It outlines the duties of assessors, the process for individuals to list taxable property, and the appointment and responsibilities of a board of supervisors who review and correct tax books. The supervisors have the authority to examine tax books, correct errors, change property valuations, swear witnesses, hear testimony, and receive tax lists of omitted individuals. They are also required to keep records of their proceedings and certify their approval of the tax book to the town clerk.

classification.summary: This act amends a previous act concerning the town of Versailles by striking out section seventy-seven under the head of "Elections" and inserting new provisions for voter eligibility. The new provisions require voters to be male citizens, twenty-one years of age, with specific residency requirements in the state and town, and to have paid their head or poll-tax for the preceding year at least thirty days before the election. It also mandates that voters must be residents of the town for sixty days prior to the election, and a list of delinquent taxpayers is to be provided to the election judge.

WHEREAS , The Paris,Hume and Bedford Turnpike Road
Company have latelyextended said turnpike road a distance

Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :

the same ishereby,so amended as toincludewithin itscorpo-
rate powers ,authorities,rights,and privileges,as a part and
portion of said turnpike road, the extension recentlymade ,

Sidner and others,to the intersectionwith the Lexington and
Cynthiana Turnpike Road near to Union Church ,in Fayette
county .
§ 2. That the said company may purchase property for a
toll-house and erecta gate,at any suitablepointon saidroad
within a half mile of itswestern terminus ,and may collectat
said gate one halfof the tollwhich is allowed by law to be
collectedat a fullgate on said road .
§ 3. That any person,company ,or corporationthat contrib-
uted to the extensionof saidturnpike road shallbe entitled
to a certificatefor one share of stock in said company for
each sum of fiftydollarspaid by them .
§ 4. That at the next annual election of officersa president
and treasurer only shallbe elected as officersand managers
of saidroad,fora term of one year,and untilthe electionof
their successors. The requirement in the charter of four
directors ishereby repealed .
§ 5. This actshallbe in forcefrom itspassage.

The commissioner is responsible for collecting and managing taxes designated for the county's sinking fund, with provisions for loaning out these funds at an interest rate not exceeding eight percent. The county court has the authority to appoint, remove, and require additional surety from the commissioner.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial management of Ballard County through a sinking fund commissioner. It outlines the administrative and financial procedures for collecting and managing taxes for debt repayment. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend and re-enact an act entitled "An Act to provide for the payment of the indebtedness of the county of Ballard, and to authorize the issue of county bonds for that purpose," approved March 15, 1878, and to authorize the issue of county bonds for the purpose of paying off the indebtedness of the county of Ballard.
classification.summary: This act details the process for issuing and managing county bonds in Ballard County, Kentucky, to pay off existing indebtedness. It outlines procedures for the county court to authorize bond issuance, for a commissioner to sell the bonds at par value, for the sheriff to collect ad valorem taxes to service the debt, and for the management of the sinking fund. It also specifies penalties for defaults by the sheriff or collector, including interest accrual.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses financial matters related to county bonds, taxes, and debt management in Ballard County, Kentucky. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The language and subject matter are purely administrative and financial. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville, Harrods Creek and Westport Railroad Company
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for a railroad company to acquire land and materials for its construction. It details the appointment of commissioners by the county court to assess damages and compensation when the company cannot reach an agreement with landowners. The statute also specifies procedures for dealing with non-resident landowners, femes covert, infants, and persons non compos mentis, including the appointment of an attorney to defend absent owners.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the legal process for eminent domain, specifically for a railroad company to acquire land and materials. It details the appointment of commissioners, the assessment of damages, and procedures for dealing with various categories of landowners, including those who are non-residents, femes covert, infants, or non compos mentis. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would inherently discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' and the year 1880, which refers to a legislative session. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on property rights and legal procedures without any racial component.

The act also mandates a standard gauge for the railroad.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to provide for the education of the colored children of the city of Owensboro
classification.summary: This act levies a special tax on property owned by persons of African descent in Owensboro to fund the education of colored children. It establishes a board of trustees to manage these funds and oversee the schools for colored children, separate from those for white children. The tax collection and fund disbursement mechanisms are outlined, mirroring those for white children's education.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'persons of African descent' and 'colored children' in the context of establishing separate educational funding and facilities. This is a clear indicator of a Jim Crow law, specifically in the realm of education. The tax is levied on one racial group to fund separate facilities for that same group, demonstrating racial segregation in public services. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as part of 'LAWS OF KENTUCKY' from a specific year (1880), indicating it was an act passed during a legislative session.

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Covington by changing the requirement for bond issuance approval. Previously, a majority of qualified voters were required to approve bond issues. This amendment changes the requirement to a majority of the votes cast at an election. It also specifies that the interest rate on bonds shall not exceed six percent per annum and they shall not be sold for less than their face value.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend and reduce into one the several acts concerning the town of Madisonville," approved February 13, 1873.
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation concerning the town of Madisonville. It establishes a board of councilmen, five in number, to be elected annually on the first Monday in August by qualified voters. Eligibility for councilman requires being at least twenty-four years old and residing in the town for six months prior to election. The councilmen will form a body corporate and politic, known as the board of councilmen of Madisonville, with the power to contract, sue, and be sued. They will hold office for one year and must take an oath to faithfully discharge their duties. The existing trustees will continue their duties until the new councilmen are elected and qualified. The councilmen will elect a president from among themselves to preside over meetings.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the municipal government of Madisonville, specifically the election and duties of town councilmen. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is solely on the administrative structure and electoral process of the town. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also grants them powers similar to town trustees and allows them to suppress specific establishments and behaviors like tippling-houses, bawdy-houses, gambling-houses, riots, and disorderly conduct.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Madisonville
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Madisonville, Kentucky. It details how individuals sentenced to confinement or fines for violating town ordinances will be handled. Prisoners will be committed to the jail of Hopkins County. The town council is authorized to hire out prisoners for labor on streets or public works, or to erect and manage a workhouse for their confinement. The terms of labor cannot exceed the terms of imprisonment. Money earned from labor goes to the town treasurer.

It outlines the process for handling convicted individuals, including their commitment to county jail, potential labor assignments, and the establishment of a workhouse. classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the town of Adairville, in Lozan county.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Adairville, Logan County. It repeals Section 35 of the original act. It specifies that non-residents must own real estate in the town for six months prior to an election to vote for trustees. It grants the board of trustees jurisdiction over circuses and exhibitions within a half-mile radius of the town, and the power to control building and improvement character for health, comfort, and safety. The trustees are also empowered to levy a tax of up to ten cents on taxable property for improvements, provided a majority of legally qualified voters approve it in an election, with specific notice requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute is an amendment to the charter of the town of Adairville. It deals with local governance, voting qualifications for non-residents, and the town's taxing and regulatory powers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The voting qualification for non-residents is based on property ownership, not race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to empower the court of claims of Shelby county to levy an additional ad valorem tax.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the court of claims of Shelby County to levy an additional ad valorem tax of up to two and one-half cents per one hundred dollars of taxable property for the years 1880 through 1884. The funds are to be used exclusively for paying the county's existing indebtedness from appropriations for turnpike and other public improvements as of February 1, 1880. The sheriff is responsible for collecting the tax and must execute a bond for its faithful discharge. The sheriff will have the same powers and penalties as for collecting state taxes, with compensation fixed by the court.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial management of Shelby County, specifically authorizing an additional tax to address existing county debt related to public improvements. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Confirm the Charter of the Maysville Water Company
classification.summary: This act confirms the corporate status of the Maysville Water Company, grants it perpetual succession, and allows it to sue and be sued, contract, and hold property necessary for its waterworks operations. It also specifies the company's capital stock and governance structure. The act ratifies a contract between the company and the city of Maysville for the perpetual use of fire hydrants and other waterworks apparatus, which had been approved by a majority of the city's voters.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the confirmation of a corporate charter for the Maysville Water Company and its contract with the city of Maysville. The text focuses entirely on the business and legal aspects of the water company's operations and its agreement with the city. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the city of Maysville.
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Maysville, granting the board of councilmen the power and duty to issue and sell coupon bonds up to $50,000 to fund payments to the Maysville Water Company for perpetual use of fire hydrants and other water-works apparatus. It also extends the city limits to include the water company's reservoir site.

It details the issuance of bonds to pay a water company for services and the extension of city limits. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of Bardstown
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Bardstown by adding a proviso to an earlier act. It requires that all persons offering to vote in any municipal election in Bardstown must have paid all town taxes assessed against them by January 31st preceding the election. Voters challenged on this basis must produce tax receipts or other evidence of payment.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to voter qualifications in municipal elections in Bardstown, specifically requiring the payment of taxes. While poll taxes and other financial requirements were often used as tools to disenfranchise Black voters during the Jim Crow era, this statute does not explicitly mention race or have any inherent racial component in its text. It applies to all voters equally. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act authorizes county courts to levy an annual tax of up to ten cents per one hundred dollars of taxable property to create a sinking fund for paying off bonded indebtedness, including principal and interest. The act also details the redemption order of bonds and the process for paying them off when sufficient funds are available.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to amend and reduce into one the several acts concerning the city of Henderson ," approved February 11th, 1867 , and the several acts amendatory thereto.
classification.summary: This act amends previous legislation concerning the city of Henderson. It grants the city council the power to levy and collect taxes annually at a rate not exceeding sixty cents per one hundred dollars of property value. It specifies that land within city limits but outside original ten-acre lots will not be taxed unless divided into five-acre lots or less. It also exempts the courthouse and public lot belonging to the county from taxation. Additionally, it repeals and replaces Section 22 of an amendment approved February 14, 1876, concerning the timing of tax book preparation and collection.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns municipal taxation and property assessment for the city of Henderson. It details tax rates, exemptions, and the process for tax collection. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Laws in Relation to the Collection of Taxes
classification.summary: This act amends existing laws regarding the collection of taxes, specifically focusing on penalties for late payments and the separation of the city marshal's duties from tax collection. It introduces a tiered penalty system for taxes not paid by specific dates, with increasing percentages added each month. It also repeals provisions that made the city marshal the ex-officio collector of taxes, establishing a separate office of tax collector effective September 1881. The new tax collector will assume the powers, duties, and penalties of the marshal in tax collection, with a commission limit of three percent on collections other than poll taxes.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the administrative process of tax collection, including penalties for late payments and the restructuring of the tax collection office. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The changes described are procedural and administrative, affecting all taxpayers equally in terms of the tax collection process. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend an Act Entitled "An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 109 of the General Statutes, Relating to the City of Henderson."
classification.summary: This act creates the office of Collector of Taxes for the city of Henderson and establishes eligibility requirements for this office. Specifically, Section 6 states that only a 'white male citizen' of Henderson, at least twenty-five years old, and a bona fide resident for at least two years prior to election, is eligible to hold the office of tax collector. The act also abolishes the office of judge of the Henderson city court and transfers its duties to the mayor.
classification.reasoning: Section 6 of the statute explicitly states that to be eligible for the office of collector of taxes, a person must be a 'white male citizen'. This is a clear racial and gender-based restriction on holding public office, which is characteristic of Jim Crow laws. classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1880 details the powers and authorizations granted to the president and directors of a company, likely a railway or industrial corporation. It covers their rights to acquire land for infrastructure (railways, telegraphs, oil pipes), erect necessary buildings and machinery for operations, and engage in mining and manufacturing. It also outlines their authority to make connections with other transportation systems, enter into contractual arrangements for freight and passenger transportation, consolidate with other companies, and lease or sell the railway. Furthermore, it permits the issuance of bonds to finance operations, secured by a mortgage on the company's assets. The text specifies the minimum denomination for bonds and the maximum amount per mile of railway.

It details rights related to land acquisition, infrastructure development, operational arrangements, and financial instruments like bonds and mortgages. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The act further outlines a process for the company to acquire land and rights-of-way through eminent domain if agreement cannot be reached with landowners.

classification.title: AN ACT to incorporate the town of Munfordville, in Hart county, Kentucky.
classification.summary: This act incorporates the town of Munfordville in Hart County, Kentucky. It defines the corporate boundaries of the town and establishes that its fiscal, municipal, and prudential concerns will be managed by five trustees elected annually by qualified voters who have resided in the town for six months. Trustees must be real estate owners in the town and have resided there for twelve months prior to election. They will serve one-year terms and have the power to fill vacancies.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act to incorporate the National Mutual Benefit Association.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act from 1878 concerning the incorporation of the National Mutual Benefit Association. It modifies section two of the original act, allowing members to be assigned to two divisions with separate accounts. The first division has a maximum of 3200 members, and vacancies are filled immediately. The second division is unlimited, and members can hold multiple certificates up to a certain value. The act also introduces seven classes for new members based on age.

classification.summary: This act amends the incorporation of the Kentucky Mutual Security Fund Company. It redefines age-based classes for members and modifies provisions related to voting by proxy and the application of mortuary fees and funds. Specifically, it adjusts the age ranges for membership classes and allows the board of directors to reduce the number of classes. It also removes the ability for absent directors to vote by proxy and adds flexibility in how death losses can be paid. The act details the process for collecting and disbursing mortuary fees based on member divisions and stipulates deductions for a permanent fund and expenses.

classification.title: AN ACT to prevent stock from running at large in Kenton county.
classification.summary: This act makes it unlawful for any person to allow horses, jacks, jennets, mules, cattle, sheep, hogs, or any other kind of stock to run at large in Kenton county. It mandates that sheriffs, deputies, or constables seize and impound any such stock found running at large, and the owner must pay all fees and costs to redeem their animals. The owner of stock found trespassing is liable for all damages and costs.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the issue of livestock running at large in Kenton County, Kentucky. It outlines regulations for impounding stray animals and holding owners liable for damages and costs. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The second section (Chapter 379) is an amendment to the charter of the Kentucky Central Railroad Company, granting it powers related to other railroad companies.
