classification.title: An Act to Amend and Re-enact Chapter 33 of the General Statutes, Relating to the Incorporation of Towns
classification.summary: This act details the election and duties of town chairmen and trustees, including residency requirements, powers to call meetings, preside, and cast tie-breaking votes. It also outlines the process for dividing towns into wards for trustee elections, specifying that wards should be as equal as practicable in population and improvement. Voters are required to vote in their own ward for ward-specific trustees and for a chairman who can be elected from any ward. The act also provides for elections to be held at a single location if wards are not established.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Georgetown
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Georgetown, Kentucky, by outlining the qualifications for voters in town elections. It specifies that male citizens over twenty-one, residing in Georgetown for six months, who have paid poll taxes, and legal voters of Scott County who have owned real property in Georgetown for six months are qualified voters. It also details election procedures, including the return of poll books, determination of results, and filling of vacancies.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to election laws in Georgetown, Kentucky. It defines voter qualifications based on age, residency, poll tax payment, and property ownership. While it sets specific criteria for voting, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race or any discriminatory provisions that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. The focus is on general civic and property requirements for voting within the town. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Georgetown
classification.summary: This act amends the charter of Georgetown, Kentucky. It clarifies definitions for 'Chairman and Board', 'Police Court', and 'Police Judge'. It also establishes that existing ordinances remain in force until changed by the board. A key provision requires voters to have paid their poll tax five days prior to an election, with penalties for non-compliance. The act also clarifies the role of the town marshal.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the municipal charter of Georgetown, Kentucky, and focuses on administrative definitions, the validity of existing ordinances, and election procedures related to poll tax payment. While poll taxes were historically used as a disenfranchisement tool, particularly against Black voters, this statute does not explicitly mention race or contain any provisions that inherently discriminate based on race. The requirement to pay a poll tax for voting was a common practice at the time, and its inclusion here does not, on its own, classify it as a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.summary: This act mandates the clerk of Ballard County to re-record deeds, mortgages, and other instruments of writing that were destroyed by fire on February 17, 1880. The clerk is entitled to a fee of fifty cents per document for recording and certifying, and is exempt from collecting state taxes on these re-recorded documents.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the administrative process of re-recording legal documents in Ballard County that were lost due to a fire. It details the duties of the county clerk and the fees associated with this process. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act grants J. M. Atherton & Co. the legal authority to construct and maintain a switch or branch railroad in Larue County, Kentucky. It retroactively legalizes their construction across public highways and requires them to maintain safe crossings and post warning signs. The act also authorizes an extension of this railroad line.

CHAPTER 411 .
AN ACT to amend the charterof the town of Brandenburg.
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1 . That the county judge of Meade county shallhave
concurrentjurisdictionwith the policejudge of Brandenburg
for allviolationsor infractionsof the ordinances or by -laws
of the town ,and all violationsof the penal laws of the State,
occurring within the corporate limits of the town ; and shall
have power to issuewarrants, enterjudgments , and award
executions, and award process for witnesses, and award com-
pulsory process when they failto attend. He shallhave
power to order a jury to be summoned in any case cogniza-
ble beforehim ,where a jury would be requiredbeforea jus-
ticeof the peace. It shallbe the duty of said county judge
to keep a record of allhis officialproceedings, a copy of
which shall be evidence ,and shall have the same effectas
recordsof justicesof the peace. Each party shallhave the
rightof appeal from alljudgments rendered by saidjudge,
in the same time and manner as appealsfrom justicesof the
peace in similarcases; and saidjudge shallhold his courts
for the trialof civilcauses quarterly, at such times as he
may designate. He shallkeep a docket or orderbook and
execution -book .
§ 2. He shallhave the same feesas are now allowed by law
to the police judge for his services as such .
§ 3. This act to take effectfrom itspassage.
Approved March 10,1880 .

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the mayor and council of Augusta to make subscriptions to the Augusta and Mt. Olivet Turnpike Road Company.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the mayor and council of Augusta, Kentucky, to subscribe up to $5,000 to the capital stock of the Augusta and Mt. Olivet Turnpike Road Company. The decision to subscribe requires a vote of the qualified voters of the city, with at least twenty days' notice. If a majority votes in favor, the mayor can make the subscription. The city is authorized to borrow money and issue bonds to pay for the subscription, with a tax levy of up to thirty cents per one hundred dollars of taxable property to cover the debt and interest.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the authorization for a city to invest in a turnpike road company. It outlines the process for making such an investment, including voter approval, borrowing money, and taxation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to prevent persons from throwing logs or any other obstructions in any of the creeks of Webster county.
classification.summary: This act makes it unlawful for any person to place logs, brush, or other obstructions into creeks in Webster county, or to obstruct the free running of their waters. Exceptions are made for damming creeks for mill purposes, erecting water-gaps and gates on farms, bridge piers or abutments, and shipping saw-logs. Violators face fines.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses environmental regulations concerning waterways in Webster County. It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The payment and bond issuance are contingent on the completion of each mile of turnpike. The act also specifies that the first mile built must be the most expensive section. It outlines the process for issuing bonds, levying taxes to pay them, and collecting these taxes. The county judge is empowered to convene justices of the peace to vote on these subscriptions. The act also includes a provision for ratification by voters.

classification.title: An Act for the relief of J. H. Armstrong, late sheriff of Carter county, and his sureties.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the Auditor of Public Accounts to provide J. H. Armstrong, former sheriff of Carter County, and his sureties with credit on judgments obtained against them for unpaid revenue from 1877 and 1878. The credit is to be applied for exonerations made by the Carter County Court, taxes of incorporated companies, and land sold for taxes that were purchased by the Commonwealth. It also allows for credit on damages and attorney's fees, provided Armstrong and his sureties pay any remaining balance owed to the state within two years. Additionally, it grants them two years to redeem lands sold upon executions.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It outlines procedures for tax assessment, collection, and the redemption and cancellation of bonds and coupons. classification.title: An Act to authorize the county court of Franklin county to fund its indebtedness
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Franklin County to reissue bonds to fund existing indebtedness, not exceeding $35,000, with interest not exceeding 6% per annum, payable annually, and a maturity of no more than fifteen years. The county court has the discretion to pay off the bonds within this term. The proceeds from the sale of these new bonds must first be applied to pay off bonds issued under a previous act from March 3, 1876. The new bonds are to be issued in denominations of at least $250. The county judge and clerk are responsible for selling the bonds and depositing the proceeds into the county treasury. Bondholders from the 1876 act are to be notified via newspaper advertisement to present their bonds for payment.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Yarnallton Turnpike Road Company
classification.summary: This statute incorporates the Yarnallton Turnpike Road Company, outlining the route of the proposed turnpike road, its capital stock, and the process for subscribing to shares and electing officers. The road is to be constructed from a junction of dirt roads near Yarnall's Station, crossing Elkhorn creek, and connecting to the old Frankfort Turnpike Road. The capital stock is set at five thousand dollars, divided into shares of fifty dollars each. A book for stock subscriptions is to be opened, and subscribers must sign an obligation to pay for their shares as required by the president and directors. Once twenty shares are subscribed, commissioners are to notify subscribers of a meeting to elect a president and four directors.

classification.summary: This act outlines the procedures for the removal and filling of vacancies for town officers in Brandenburg, Kentucky. It specifies that elected officers can only be removed for cause after being furnished with charges and heard in their defense. Appointed officers can be removed by a majority vote of the trustees. It also details the process for resolving ties in elections.

classification.title: An Act to Establish the Tompkinsville Police Court
classification.summary: This statute establishes the Tompkinsville Police Court, outlining its exclusive and original jurisdiction over violations of town by-laws and ordinances, and concurrent jurisdiction with county magistrates over certain Commonwealth pleas within the town limits. It details the court's powers, including taking recognizances, enforcing judgments, and acting as an examining court. The police judge is granted jurisdiction in civil cases equivalent to a justice of the peace or quarterly judge, and powers to grant injunctions, restraining orders, and writs of habeas corpus. The judge also has jurisdiction in criminal cases of misdemeanor and offenses against town by-laws, with the authority to summon juries and witnesses.

classification.title: An Act to amend an act entitled "An Act to establish a Police Court in the town of Munfordville, in Hart county," approved March 11, 1870.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for the police court in Munfordville, Hart County, Kentucky. It details how fines are to be paid, including the option of hard labor for the town at a rate of one dollar per day, with a maximum of eight hours of labor per day. It also specifies the confinement of defendants in the county jail when not laboring and outlines the appeal process from the police court to the Monroe quarterly or circuit courts based on the fine amount or imprisonment duration. The statute also states that the police court shall be open for business and have concurrent jurisdiction with justices of the peace and the quarterly judge in certain districts.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the functioning of a police court, including the imposition of fines and the option of hard labor as a means of payment. While the use of hard labor for debt or fines was a common feature of penal systems that disproportionately affected Black individuals during the Jim Crow era, this statute does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or restrictions. It applies to all defendants brought before the police court, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an amendment to a previous act passed by the General Assembly. The confidence is high because the text clearly defines the legal procedures without any racial component.

classification.title: LAWS OF KENTUCKY
classification.summary: This section of the Laws of Kentucky from 1880 details the organization and powers of a board of trustees for a town, including their meeting procedures, financial management, property control, and the ability to enact by-laws. Specifically, it grants them the power to "restrain and prohibit all description of gaming" and to "license, tax, regulate, suppress, and prohibit billiard-tables, pigeon-holes, Jenny Lind, and all similar tables, pin-alleys, nine or ten-pin alleys, ballalleys, and shooting galleries."
classification.reasoning: The provided text describes the general powers of a town's board of trustees, focusing on the regulation of public order and entertainment, such as gaming and various types of recreational establishments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

These powers include regulating various exhibitions and amusements, issuing licenses, preventing public disturbances, suppressing disorderly houses, preventing obscene conduct, and regulating innkeepers. It also grants the board the authority to create and enforce ordinances, by-laws, and police regulations through fines and imprisonment.

It covers a range of civic regulations such as licensing shows, preventing disturbances, and managing inns. The board is also responsible for keeping streets in good order, with penalties for failure.

classification.title: AN ACT to regulate civil proceedings in actions in the Daviess circuit court.
classification.summary: This act allows for the filing of pleadings, amended pleadings, cross-pleadings, demurrers, replies, rejoinders, sur-rejoinders, rebutters, sur-rebutters, responses, and other pleadings in the clerk's office of the Daviess circuit court during vacation, with the same effect as if filed in open court. It also empowers the clerk to issue any process on amended or cross-pleadings in vacation that the court could order when in session, and to make preparatory orders in vacation, such as orders of survey, revivor, appointing guardians ad litem, referring actions to the master commissioner for account settlements, estate settlements, and to report liabilities and assets of deceased persons.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to civil procedure in the Daviess circuit court, specifically regarding the filing of legal documents and the issuance of orders by the clerk during vacation periods. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Before exercising this power, the board must submit the question to the voters of the town. If a majority of qualified voters approve, the board can proceed with the subscription and issue bonds, not exceeding five thousand dollars, to pay for the stock. The bonds are to be sold at not less than par and have denominations between one hundred and one thousand dollars, with a maturity not exceeding ten years.

classification.summary: This act incorporates "The Young Ladies' Academy of the Visitation, Mount Admirabilis" as a body politic. If a majority votes in favor, the general council is mandated to levy the increased tax.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Louisville, Cincinnati and Lexington Railway Company
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for a company, likely a railroad, to acquire property through eminent domain. It details the procedures for summoning a jury, valuing property, and considering consequential damages and benefits. The process involves a justice of the peace, sheriff, and a jury of twenty inhabitants who are not related or interested in the property. The jury's decision is then returned to the circuit court for confirmation. The statute also specifies how to handle situations where property owners are femes covert, minors, non compos mentis, or out of the county.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the legal process for eminent domain, which is a civil procedure for acquiring private property for public use. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. The procedures described are general and apply to all property owners regardless of their race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to authorize the county court of Anderson county to levy and collect an ad valorem tax in said county.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the county court of Anderson County to levy and collect an annual ad valorem tax, not exceeding twenty-five cents per one hundred dollars worth of property, in addition to the existing poll-tax. The funds raised are to be used for paying the interest and principal of the county's outstanding debt and for current yearly expenses. The county court may also appropriate portions of this tax for bridge construction and repairing, and for constructing fire-proof vaults in circuit and county court clerk's offices. The tax is to be collected by the sheriff under the same responsibilities as the county levy tax.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial management of Anderson County, specifically authorizing an additional ad valorem tax to cover expenses and debt. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is purely on local taxation and fiscal responsibility. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Amend the Charter of the Town of Vanceburg
classification.summary: This act details various powers granted to the board of trustees of the town of Vanceburg, including the ability to license, tax, regulate, suppress, and prohibit certain businesses and activities such as billiard tables, shooting galleries, and shows. It also outlines procedures for collecting fines and forfeitures and appropriating town funds. Notably, it prohibits individuals who sell spirituous, vinous, or malt liquors from holding the office of trustee or police judge. The act also empowers the board to direct pavement construction on main streets.

Property owners are responsible for the cost, and if they fail to comply, the board can undertake the work and assess the expenses, collecting them as taxes. The act also details the appointment and duties of an assessor, the process for levying and collecting taxes and assessments, and establishes these as liens on property. It outlines procedures for tax sales and redemption.

It also allows the company to sue and be sued, contract, and make by-laws, with the exclusion of banking privileges.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation of a race and fair company. It outlines the company's name, corporators, purpose (improving breeds of horses and stock), and powers (purchasing/leasing property, holding races and fairs, suing and being sued). There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Big Spring Manufacturing Company
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Big Spring Manufacturing Company, outlining its capital stock, business management, officer appointments, and procedures for stock subscriptions and transfers. It specifies the company's capital stock limit, share value, payment installments, and forfeiture conditions for non-payment. The management structure includes a president, vice-president, a board of seven directors, a secretary, and a treasurer. The act also details the process for filling vacancies on the board and the initial election of officers. It empowers the president and directors to open subscription books and defines the rights of shareholders, including the requirement for a vote of the directors for a new member to be admitted upon purchasing a share from an existing member.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the incorporation of a manufacturing company, including its capital structure, management, and operational procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute focuses solely on the business and organizational aspects of the company. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is 'other' as it does not fit any of the specific Jim Crow categories. The racial indicator is 'none'.

classification.title: An Act to Incorporate the Kentucky Benevolent Association
classification.summary: This act incorporates the Kentucky Benevolent Association, a corporation whose object is to create a beneficiary fund for the families or relatives of deceased members. It outlines the principal place of business, the organization and management of the board of directors, and the powers of the board in determining membership, setting dues, and managing funds. A specific requirement for board members is that they must be members of some Methodist denomination of Christians.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the incorporation of a benevolent association for the purpose of providing a beneficiary fund. It details the organizational structure, management, and operational powers of the corporation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The requirements for board membership are religious (Methodist denomination), not racial. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the rules for a member-based association, detailing how funds are paid upon a member's death, the protection of these funds from creditors, admission fees, annual dues, and assessments upon death. It also specifies the process for notifying members of a death, collecting assessments, and the consequences of non-payment, including forfeiture of rights and reinstatement procedures. The statute also defines the legal notice of death and the timeframe for claiming benefits, as well as the maximum payout to beneficiaries.

classification.summary: This act incorporates "The Kenton County Gunning and Fishing Club" as a body-corporate. It grants the club perpetual succession, the right to hold property up to $25,000, make contracts, sue and be sued, and adopt a constitution and by-laws. The club's objectives are to protect and increase game and fish, enforce related laws, and promote field sports. It also has the power to shoot and fish on its premises, and members are not personally liable for the society's debts. The county judge can appoint police to maintain order on the premises. The act takes effect upon passage.

classification.title: An Act to Authorize the President of the Joint Stock Agricultural and Mechanical Association of Madison County to Borrow Money and Mortgage Property
classification.summary: This act empowers the president of the Joint Stock Agricultural and Mechanical Association of Madison County to borrow money and mortgage the association's real and personal property to cover existing or future debts. It grants the company power to construct, maintain, and operate a railway, validates a contract for lease, construction, and operation with the Louisville, Cincinnati, and Lexington Railway Company, and sets the number of directors to be elected. A proviso requires two directors to live north of Shelbyville to represent stockholders along that line.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the reorganization and operational validation of a railroad company. It does not contain any language, provisions, or implications that suggest racial segregation, discrimination, or any other aspect of Jim Crow laws. The mention of specific geographic representation for directors (north of Shelbyville) is related to business operations and stockholder representation, not race.

CHAPTER 630 .
AN ACT to prohibitthe sale of spirituous, vinous, or malt liquorsin the
town of Morehead , in Rowan county .
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That hereafterit shallbe unlawfulfor any person or
persons to sell, vend ,loan,or furnish spirituous,vinous, or
malt liquorsin the town of Morehead ,in Rowan county .
§ 2. That it shallbe the duty of the policejudge and the
trusteesof saidtown ,upon the applicationof fiveof the cit-
izens of said town ,to cause an election to be held in said
town within twenty days after said application, at which
electioneach voter shallbe plainlyasked " Do you vote foror
againstthe saleofliquorsin the town of Morehead ?" and the
firstsectionof thisact shallnot be in forceuntila majority
ofthe votersof saidtown votingshallbe castagainstthe
sale of liquors.
§ 3. That any person violatingthe provisionsof thisact
shall be deemed guilty of a misdemeanor , and fined, upon
indictmentin the circuitcourt,in the sum of sixtydollars.
§ 4. This act to take effectfrom itspassage.
Approved March 29, 1880.
CHAPTER 631 .

this act shallbe liable, on conviction thereof, to a fineof not
less than five dollars nor more than ten dollars for each

any magistrateor policejudge in saidcounty.
§ 3. This act to takeeffectfrom and afterthe firstday of
March ,1880 .
Approved March 29, 1880 .

classification.summary: This act amends the charter of the city of Lexington by establishing a new schedule for mayoral and other city official elections. It specifies that an election for mayor will be held on the first Saturday in March 1881, for a one-year term. Subsequently, on the first Saturday in March 1882, and biennially thereafter, elections will be held for mayor, collector, assessor, auditor, treasurer, attorney, physician, surveyor, and keeper of the poor and workhouse.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend the charter of the city of Louisville.
classification.summary: This act authorizes the city of Louisville to issue $1,000,000 in coupon bonds to pay off its floating debt existing as of January 1, 1879. The bonds will bear 5% interest and have varying maturity dates. The city council is directed to levy an annual tax of ten cents on each $100 worth of property to cover principal and interest payments. The proceeds from taxes levied prior to 1879 (excluding those for public schools and house of refuge) will also be paid into the sinking fund for bonded debt payment. The mayor will deliver the bonds to the sinking fund commissioners for sale.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial management of the city of Louisville, specifically authorizing the issuance of bonds to cover a floating debt and establishing a tax to repay these bonds. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute is purely financial and administrative in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This act prohibits the sale, bartering, giving, or loaning of spirituous, malt, or vinous liquors within school district No. 17, located in Hickman County, Kentucky, effective May 1, 1880. CHAPTER 669 .
AN ACT to prohibitstock from running at largein Hancock county.
Be itenactedby theGeneralAssemblyof theCommonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. Any person,being the owner or having the custody or
controlof any stock of any kind,in Hancock county, who
shallknowingly sufferor permit saidstockto run at largein

under his charge ,unless under the care of some competent
attendant,shallbe finedin any amount not lessthan one dol-
larnor more than twenty dollars,to be recoveredby warrant
in the name of the Commonwealth before any justiceof the
peace .

jured by stock running at large in said county, with the

such stock,notwithstandingthe premises or property so in-
jured may not be inclosedby a lawfulfence,as now required
by law, for any damages he may have sustained,and may
alsohave a lienupon the stock so trespassingto satisfyany
judgment he may recover,and may alsohave executionlevied
upon saidstock,and the same soldin satisfactionthereof,not-
withstanding itmay be exempt from saidlevy and saleunder
the present law .
§3 Any person findingany stock upon his premises,or on
premises under his control,shallhave the right to take and
hold the same as estraysare now taken up and held.

classification.title: AN ACT to prohibit stock from running at large in Hancock county.
classification.summary: This act prohibits livestock from running at large in Hancock County, Kentucky. Owners or custodians of livestock are subject to fines if their animals are found without enclosure or competent attendant. Property owners injured by trespassing livestock have a right of action against the owner, regardless of whether their property is lawfully fenced, and can place a lien on the trespassing stock to satisfy any judgment. Individuals finding stock on their premises can hold it as estrays.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns regulations for livestock in Hancock County, Kentucky. It outlines penalties for allowing stock to run at large and provides remedies for property owners whose land is damaged by trespassing animals. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend an act, entitled "An act to incorporate the Southern Baptist Theological Seminary," approved February 15, 1876.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous act incorporating the Southern Baptist Theological Seminary. It specifies that contributions and donations for an endowment fund made after February 1st, 1880, shall be held sacred and inviolate. The principal cannot be used, only the interest, income, and dividends. If the trustees or financial board mismanage the fund, it will revert to the donors. Once $200,000 is secured in cash or government/state/corporation bonds, a financial board of five members of a regular Baptist Church, a majority of whom must be citizens of Louisville, Kentucky, will manage the seminary's financial affairs. This board will be elected annually by donors who have contributed $1,000 or more.

CHAPTER 682 .
AN ACT for the benefit of James S. Wise .
WHEREAS , Dr. James S. Wise ,of Covington,at the request
of Governor McCreary , and under appointment of Captain
Frank Wood , of the Covington Light Guards , accompanied
said company of Statemilitiain the capacityof surgeon and
physician, to Carter county, Kentucky , in the summer of
1877,which said militiawas sent by the then Governor of
this Commonwealth to saidcounty to suppressthe insurrec-
tionthen existingin saidcounty,known as the "Underwood
war ;" and whereas , said Dr. James S. Wise has never re-
ceived any compensation forservicesrendered by him on said
occasion ; therefore,
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That saidDr. James S. Wise ,out of any money in the
Treasury not otherwise appropriated,forhis said servicesbe
allowed the sum of ninetydollars,and the Auditor be,and
he ishereby,instructedtodraw hiswarrant on the Treasurer
forsaidsum of ninety dollars.
§ 2. This act to take effectfrom itspassage.
Approved April 1,1880.

forpayment ,at a rateof interestnot exceeding sixper cent.

isentirelypaid.

AN ACT forthe benefitErnestTyler, of Shelby county .

CHAPTER 691 .
AN ACT forthe benefitof Timothy Ford ,of Garrard county .
WHEREAS , At the February term , 1879 , of the Garrard cir-
cuitcourt, Timothy Ford was convictedof sending a chal-
lengeto fighta duel:
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the provision of chapter 29, article20, of the
General Statutes, so far as the same abridges the right of
said Ford to vote,be,and the same is hereby,declaredvoid.
§ 2. This actshalltake effectfrom itspassage.
Approved April 1 , 1880.

CHAPTER 692 .

arms and equipments,not exceeding sixty stand of arms , to the graded
and militarypublicschoolat Harrodsburg .
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§1. That the Adjutant General of the Stateof Kentucky ,
by and with the advice and consentof the Governor , be,and
he is hereby, authorizedand empowered to furnish to the
principalof the graded and militarypublic school,situatein
Harrodsburg ,in Mercer county ,any number of small arms ,
riflesor muskets , with proper and usual equipments and
accoutrements ,not exceeding sixty stand of arms ,to be used
in educating the pupilsof said schoolin acquiringa knowl-
edge of the militaryart and scienceby drilling, and to be
used forotherpurposes not contraryto law .
§ 2. That said arms ,accoutrements,and equipments shall
be furnished to the principalof said school upon his appli-
cation therefor, in writing, and upon his executing bond ,
with approved security, for the care, safety, and return of
saidguns and accoutrements to the proper State authorities,
whenever the same may be required to be returned by the
Governor ,or whenever the publicnecessitiesrequirethatsaid
arms shallbe returnedtotheproper authoritieshaving charge
of the arms of the State .
§ 3. This act to take effectfrom itspassage.
Approved April 1, 1880.

CHAPTER 695 .
AN ACT forthe benefitof M. Rice,common school commissioner of Hop-
kins county .
WHEREAS , The school trustees of districtNo. -, of the
colored schoolsin Hopkins county, committed a mistake in
reporting the number of colored childrenof pupil age,by
which the common school commissioner failed to draw the
due proportion of the school fund; and whereas,it appears
that said commissioner should have drawn for eighty-three
pupils instead of twenty four,the amount actuallyreceived;
therefore, to remedy said mistake ,
Be itenactedby theGeneral Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
§ 1. That the Superintendent of Public Instruction is here-
by authorized to draw his warrant on the Auditor for the sum
of twenty-eightdollarsand thirty-two cents,in favor of M.
Rice, school commissioner of Hopkins county,forthe benefit
of the teacher of the colored school in Hopkins county,to
be paid out of any school fund forcolored personsdue or to
the creditof Hopkins county .
§ 2. This actshalltake effectfrom itspassage.
Approved April 1, 1880.

The amendments focus on the legal process and penalties rather than explicitly mentioning race.
